content.text: Coordinating a fleet of autonomous mobile robots is a distributed systems problem disguised as a robotics problem. When ten AMRs converge on the same aisle, they are not just navigation challenges — they are competing processes contending for shared resources, producing deadlocks and race conditions that would be familiar to any operating systems engineer. Fleet coordination is the discipline of making that competition orderly and efficient.

Fleet coordination encompasses the algorithms and infrastructure that assign tasks to robots, route them through shared space without conflict, detect and resolve deadlocks, and maintain acceptable throughput as the fleet scales. It spans three tightly coupled sub-problems:
(how robots traverse shared infrastructure without collision or gridlock), and
(the software layer that monitors, commands, and adapts the fleet in real time).

The challenge is that these sub-problems interact. An optimal task assignment may produce a path plan that deadlocks; a deadlock-free route assignment may waste time by routing robots far from their tasks. Production fleet coordination systems must solve all three simultaneously or at least iterate between them quickly enough that the combined result is acceptable.

Warehouse automation is the most visible application: facilities like Amazon, Ocado, and Geek+ operate thousands of AMRs in spaces designed around robots. A single deadlock in a high-density warehouse can cascade — one blocked robot blocks the one behind it, which blocks the one behind that — causing facility-wide throughput collapse within minutes. Fleet coordination software is the difference between a 200-robot warehouse running at design capacity and the same robots producing less throughput than a team of humans.

Beyond warehouses: autonomous port logistics, hospital medication delivery, and automotive assembly plants all deploy multi-robot fleets where coordination quality directly determines economic outcome. The market for autonomous mobile robots was valued at over $3 billion in 2023 and is growing at 20%+ annually; the software coordinating those robots is a correspondingly critical competency.
Maximizes fleet utilization by assigning tasks to the best-positioned, best-resourced robots rather than first-available
Eliminates deadlocks through proactive conflict detection before routes are executed
Provides a single source of truth for all robot locations and task states, enabling human operators to monitor and intervene
Enables heterogeneous fleets — different robot types with different capabilities — to cooperate on shared tasks
Supports dynamic re-prioritization: high-urgency orders can pre-empt lower-priority tasks without human intervention
Scales horizontally: centralized FMS can manage hundreds of robots; decentralized approaches scale further
Creates audit trails for task completion times, failure events, and throughput metrics
centralized FMS has global visibility but is a single point of failure and a communication bottleneck; decentralized approaches are resilient but can produce suboptimal global allocations
optimal multi-robot task allocation is NP-hard; practical systems use auction heuristics or greedy assignment that sacrifice global optimality for speed
all coordination architectures require reliable robot-to-FMS communication; wireless drop-outs cause state divergence
route planners assume a known environment; temporary obstacles (humans, misplaced pallets) cause plans to fail in execution
mixing robots from different vendors with different APIs is a systems integration challenge that no single standard has fully solved

The three sub-problems run on different timescales. Task allocation runs on the order of seconds, re-running whenever a new task arrives or a robot becomes free. Route planning runs on the order of hundreds of milliseconds, generating conflict-free space-time reservations for each robot's planned path. Local execution runs on the robot at 10–50 Hz, tracking the plan and reporting deviations to the FMS.
The dominant task allocation approach in industry is the

: the FMS announces a task, each robot submits a bid (typically: estimated time to complete, accounting for travel time and current queue), and the FMS awards the task to the lowest bidder. For tasks that must be bundled (e.g., pick and place at two locations) the
extends single-item auctions to multi-task bundles with a distributed consensus phase that resolves conflicting assignments between robots.
Conflict-free routing is typically handled by a variant of
, which computes paths for all robots jointly such that no two robots occupy the same location at the same time. The

algorithm solves MAPF optimally for small fleets (under ~50 robots) using a two-level search: a high-level constraint tree resolves conflicts; low-level A* plans individual paths under those constraints. Larger fleets use ECBS (enhanced CBS with suboptimality bound) or rule-based flow control (one-way corridors, token-based access to intersections).

attrs.alt: Fleet coordination architecture showing FMS at top with Auction Engine, Conflict-Free Router, and Deadlock Manager components, connected to a row of AMR robots with task assignments and status indicators

content.text: The Fleet Management System sits at the centre of the architecture. The Auction Engine handles task-to-robot assignment using market-based bidding (CBBA for bundles). The Conflict-Free Router computes space-time reservations using MAPF, issuing route instructions to each robot. The Deadlock Manager continuously monitors the Resource Allocation Graph and pre-empts the lowest-cost robot when a cycle is detected. Robots run ROS2 nodes under per-robot namespaces (
) and publish pose, task status, and battery state to the FMS via DDS.
Any deployment with 3 or more robots sharing the same physical space and infrastructure
Warehouse automation with concurrent pick, drop, and charging tasks — task allocation ROI is measurable immediately
Manufacturing lines where robots follow fixed routes but compete for intersection access tokens
Hospital logistics where delivery urgency varies and robots must respect sterile zone access controls
Outdoor autonomous delivery fleets coordinating shared road segments or charging stations
Multi-robot inspection missions where coverage goals must be divided without redundant overlap

For a new fleet, start with a single-item auction over a centralized FMS. The implementation is straightforward: maintain a task queue, a robot state table (position, battery, current task), and a bid-evaluation function that computes time-to-start for each robot-task pair. Award to the minimum-bid robot. This alone will outperform naive round-robin assignment for non-uniform task distributions.

Add conflict detection once the basic auction is working. The simplest approach is space-time reservation: each robot's planned path is a sequence of (cell, timestep) tuples; the FMS checks new path proposals against existing reservations and re-routes if a conflict is found. For ROS2 deployments, the
provides a starting point, and commercial options (MiR Fleet, Fetch Robotics FetchCore) implement full MAPF-based routing.

import heapq
import math
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Dict, Optional

@dataclass
class Task:
    task_id: str
    location: tuple          # (x, y) in meters
    priority: float = 1.0    # higher = more urgent
    estimated_duration: float = 60.0  # seconds

@dataclass
class Robot:
    robot_id: str
    position: tuple          # (x, y) in meters
    battery_pct: float       # 0-100
    current_task: Optional[str] = None
    scheduled_tasks: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def travel_time(self, destination: tuple, speed_mps: float = 1.5) -> float:
        """Euclidean distance / speed (seconds)."""
        dx = self.position[0] - destination[0]
        dy = self.position[1] - destination[1]
        return math.hypot(dx, dy) / speed_mps


class SingleItemAuction:
    """
    First-price sealed-bid auction for multi-robot task allocation.
    Each robot submits a bid reflecting its marginal cost to add the task.
    Lowest-cost bid wins (minimizes time-to-task-completion).
    """

    def __init__(self, robots: List[Robot]):
        self.robots = {r.robot_id: r for r in robots}

    def _compute_bid(self, robot: Robot, task: Task) -> float:
        """
        Bid = time until robot can start task + travel time to task.
        Lower bid = sooner the task gets done.
        Penalty applied for low battery to avoid dispatching robots that
        would need to recharge mid-task.
        """
        # Time until robot finishes its current queue
        backlog = sum(
            self.robots[robot.robot_id].scheduled_tasks.count(t) * 60
            for t in robot.scheduled_tasks
        ) if robot.scheduled_tasks else 0.0

        travel = robot.travel_time(task.location)
        battery_penalty = max(0, (30 - robot.battery_pct)) * 5.0  # penalty below 30%
        return backlog + travel + battery_penalty

    def run_auction(self, task: Task) -> Optional[str]:
        """
        Broadcast task, collect bids, award to lowest bidder.
        Returns the winning robot_id, or None if no robot is available.
        """
        bids = []
        for robot in self.robots.values():
            if robot.battery_pct < 10:          # cannot bid if nearly dead
                continue
            cost = self._compute_bid(robot, task)
            bids.append((cost, robot.robot_id))

        if not bids:
            return None

        bids.sort()
        winner_id = bids[0][1]
        winner = self.robots[winner_id]
        winner.scheduled_tasks.append(task.task_id)
        print(f"  Task {task.task_id} → {winner_id} (bid={bids[0][0]:.1f}s)")
        return winner_id

    def allocate_all(self, tasks: List[Task]) -> Dict[str, str]:
        """
        Allocate a list of tasks sequentially.
        Tasks sorted by priority (descending) before auctioning.
        """
        sorted_tasks = sorted(tasks, key=lambda t: -t.priority)
        assignments = {}
        for task in sorted_tasks:
            winner = self.run_auction(task)
            if winner:
                assignments[task.task_id] = winner
            else:
                print(f"  WARNING: no robot available for {task.task_id}")
        return assignments


# --- Example usage ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    robots = [
        Robot("amr01", position=(0, 0),   battery_pct=85),
        Robot("amr02", position=(10, 5),  battery_pct=62),
        Robot("amr03", position=(20, 0),  battery_pct=25),   # low battery
        Robot("amr04", position=(5, 15),  battery_pct=90),
    ]

    tasks = [
        Task("T001", location=(8, 8),   priority=2.0),
        Task("T002", location=(15, 3),  priority=1.0),
        Task("T003", location=(2, 12),  priority=1.5),
        Task("T004", location=(18, 14), priority=1.0),
    ]

    auctioneer = SingleItemAuction(robots)
    result = auctioneer.allocate_all(tasks)
    print("\nFinal assignments:", result)
CBBA extends the single-item auction to let each robot build a bundle of multiple tasks, then reach consensus via message passing:

import numpy as np
from copy import deepcopy
from typing import List, Dict, Tuple

class CBBAAgent:
    """
    Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA) agent.
    Choi, How, & Brunet, IEEE TRO 2009.

    Each agent maintains:
      - bundle: ordered list of tasks it has assigned to itself
      - path: same tasks in execution order
      - winning_bid[j]: highest bid seen for task j
      - winning_agent[j]: agent that placed that bid
    """

    def __init__(self, agent_id: int, position: np.ndarray,
                 n_tasks: int, max_bundle: int = 4):
        self.id = agent_id
        self.position = position
        self.n_tasks = n_tasks
        self.max_bundle = max_bundle

        self.bundle: List[int] = []          # task indices in bundle
        self.path: List[int] = []
        self.winning_bid: np.ndarray = np.zeros(n_tasks)
        self.winning_agent: np.ndarray = np.full(n_tasks, -1, dtype=int)

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Phase 1: Bundle construction
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def _score(self, task_idx: int, task_positions: np.ndarray) -> float:
        """
        Marginal score for adding task_idx to the current bundle.
        Here: negative travel distance (maximize negative distance = minimize distance).
        Replace with mission-specific utility in production.
        """
        if self.path:
            last = task_positions[self.path[-1]]
        else:
            last = self.position
        dist = np.linalg.norm(task_positions[task_idx] - last)
        return -dist   # higher is better

    def build_bundle(self, task_positions: np.ndarray):
        """Greedily add tasks until bundle is full or no improvement."""
        while len(self.bundle) < self.max_bundle:
            best_score = -np.inf
            best_task = -1
            for j in range(self.n_tasks):
                if j in self.bundle:
                    continue
                score = self._score(j, task_positions)
                bid_value = max(score, 0)
                if bid_value > self.winning_bid[j] and score > best_score:
                    best_score = score
                    best_task = j
            if best_task == -1:
                break
            self.bundle.append(best_task)
            self.path.append(best_task)
            self.winning_bid[best_task] = max(best_score, 0)
            self.winning_agent[best_task] = self.id

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Phase 2: Consensus (one round of message passing with neighbor)
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def receive_message(self, sender_id: int, sender_bids: np.ndarray,
                        sender_agents: np.ndarray):
        """
        Update local winning bids based on neighbor's broadcast.
        Tasks won by the neighbor at higher bid → release from own bundle.
        """
        release = []
        for j in range(self.n_tasks):
            if sender_bids[j] > self.winning_bid[j]:
                self.winning_bid[j] = sender_bids[j]
                self.winning_agent[j] = sender_agents[j]
                if j in self.bundle:
                    release.append(j)
        # Remove released tasks and all tasks behind them in bundle
        if release:
            first_release = min(self.bundle.index(r) for r in release)
            self.bundle = self.bundle[:first_release]
            self.path = self.path[:first_release]


def run_cbba(n_agents: int, task_positions: np.ndarray,
             consensus_rounds: int = 10) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
    """Run CBBA to convergence."""
    agents = [
        CBBAAgent(i, np.random.rand(2) * 20, len(task_positions))
        for i in range(n_agents)
    ]

    for _ in range(consensus_rounds):
        for agent in agents:
            agent.build_bundle(task_positions)
        # Broadcast to all neighbors (fully connected here)
        for agent in agents:
            for other in agents:
                if other.id != agent.id:
                    agent.receive_message(other.id, other.winning_bid.copy(),
                                          other.winning_agent.copy())

    return {a.id: a.path for a in agents}


# Quick test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    np.random.seed(0)
    tasks = np.random.rand(12, 2) * 20    # 12 random task positions
    assignment = run_cbba(n_agents=4, task_positions=tasks, consensus_rounds=8)
    for agent_id, task_list in assignment.items():
        print(f"  Agent {agent_id}: tasks {task_list}")
Deadlock detection using a Resource Allocation Graph with DFS cycle detection:

from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Dict, Set, Optional, List

class ResourceAllocationGraph:
    """
    Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) for deadlock detection in multi-robot fleets.
    
    Nodes:
      - Robot nodes (R_i): labeled "robot:<id>"
      - Resource nodes (L_j): labeled "loc:<x>,<y>" or "corridor:<id>"
    
    Edges:
      - R_i → L_j: robot i is REQUESTING resource j (waiting)
      - L_j → R_i: resource j is HELD BY robot i
    
    A cycle in the RAG indicates a deadlock.
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self.held_by: Dict[str, str] = {}           # resource → robot_id
        self.waiting_for: Dict[str, str] = {}        # robot_id → resource
        self.robot_holds: Dict[str, Set[str]] = defaultdict(set)  # robot → set of resources

    def acquire(self, robot_id: str, resource: str) -> bool:
        """Try to acquire resource. Returns True if granted immediately."""
        if resource not in self.held_by:
            self.held_by[resource] = robot_id
            self.robot_holds[robot_id].add(resource)
            return True
        # Resource is held by another robot — register wait edge
        self.waiting_for[robot_id] = resource
        return False

    def release(self, robot_id: str, resource: str):
        """Release a held resource."""
        self.held_by.pop(resource, None)
        self.robot_holds[robot_id].discard(resource)
        # Notify any robot waiting for this resource
        for r, res in list(self.waiting_for.items()):
            if res == resource:
                del self.waiting_for[r]
                self.acquire(r, resource)   # grant to first waiter
                break

    def detect_deadlock(self) -> Optional[List[str]]:
        """
        DFS cycle detection in the wait-for graph.
        Returns the cycle as a list of robot IDs, or None if no deadlock.
        """
        visited: Set[str] = set()
        rec_stack: Set[str] = set()

        def dfs(robot: str) -> Optional[List[str]]:
            visited.add(robot)
            rec_stack.add(robot)
            resource = self.waiting_for.get(robot)
            if resource:
                blocker = self.held_by.get(resource)
                if blocker and blocker != robot:
                    if blocker not in visited:
                        result = dfs(blocker)
                        if result is not None:
                            return [robot] + result
                    elif blocker in rec_stack:
                        return [robot, blocker]   # cycle found
            rec_stack.discard(robot)
            return None

        for robot in list(self.waiting_for.keys()):
            if robot not in visited:
                cycle = dfs(robot)
                if cycle:
                    return cycle
        return None

    def resolve_deadlock(self, cycle: List[str]) -> str:
        """
        Pre-empt the robot with fewest held resources (least cost to backoff).
        In production: use priority, task urgency, or battery level.
        """
        victim = min(cycle, key=lambda r: len(self.robot_holds[r]))
        # Release all resources held by victim
        for resource in list(self.robot_holds[victim]):
            self.release(victim, resource)
        if victim in self.waiting_for:
            del self.waiting_for[victim]
        print(f"  Deadlock resolved: pre-empting robot {victim}")
        return victim

Ocado's Customer Fulfilment Centres (CFCs) run grids of approximately 1,000 robots per facility, picking grocery orders. Each robot is a cube-shaped bot that moves on a 3D grid; the coordination system assigns pick tasks via an auction that minimizes path length, plans routes using a proprietary MAPF variant, and manages charging cycles automatically. The system maintains 99.9%+ uptime through redundant FMS instances and per-robot local fallback behaviours that allow continued task execution during brief communication losses.

The key engineering insight from Ocado's design: the grid architecture (fixed infrastructure, controlled traffic flow) dramatically simplifies MAPF. Each cell can hold exactly one robot; cell access is controlled by the FMS via time-slot reservations; and the grid's regularity allows heuristic MAPF solvers to find near-optimal solutions in milliseconds even for 1,000 robots. Unstructured environments (warehouses with arbitrary shelf layouts and human co-workers) are harder because the state space is continuous and the environment is partially dynamic.
Re-running the global auction from scratch on every new task arrival — instead, use incremental assignment: only re-evaluate tasks whose winning robot has become unavailable
Ignoring robot battery state in bid computation — a robot with 8% battery will accept a task it cannot complete before needing to charge
Using Euclidean distance as the bid metric instead of actual path length — two robots may have the same straight-line distance to a task but very different path costs given the actual map
Designing the FMS as a single-threaded request handler — at fleet sizes above ~20 robots, synchronous handling introduces scheduling latency that reduces throughput
Relying on timeout-based deadlock detection — timeouts are too slow for high-throughput operations; use proactive RAG cycle detection
Forgetting to reserve space for robot turning radius — a plan that looks conflict-free for point robots collides when robots have physical footprints
Not simulating deadlock scenarios in staging — deadlocks only emerge under specific traffic patterns and are often invisible in low-density testing
Separate the task allocator from the path planner — they run at different rates and must be independently testable; use a message bus (ROS2 topics, MQTT) between them
Store all task assignments and robot states in a persistent log — when the FMS restarts after failure it can reconstruct in-progress work rather than starting from scratch
Implement a local fallback mode on each robot: if FMS communication is lost for more than 5 seconds, the robot completes its current segment and then stops safely rather than continuing blind
Use a map version hash — when the map is updated (new shelf added, emergency exit blocked), invalidate all in-flight route plans and require re-planning
Add priority lanes to high-throughput corridors and ensure the path planner respects them — a robot on a low-priority task should never block a corridor used by high-priority throughput routes
Test CBBA with up to 20% of robots offline to verify the remaining fleet re-allocates work correctly

Single-item auction scales to hundreds of tasks per second on a modern server — the per-task bid evaluation is O(R) where R is the number of robots. CBBA consensus adds O(R² · T) per round where T is the number of tasks in bundles; for fleets under 100 robots and bundles under 6 tasks this runs in under 100 ms.

MAPF via CBS is exponential in the worst case but practical for fleets under ~50 robots in structured environments. For larger fleets, prioritized planning (plan robots in priority order, treating higher-priority robots as moving obstacles for lower-priority ones) runs in O(R · A*) time at the cost of suboptimality. Production systems at Ocado and Amazon use proprietary heuristic MAPF variants that exploit grid structure to solve 1,000-robot instances in real time.
Launch with robot_namespace param; share maps via map_server
C++ library; import via CMake; good for benchmarking
Auction + MAPF; REST API for custom integration
Open fleet management for heterogeneous robots
Apache 2.0; supports multi-vendor fleets via adapters
VRP solver usable as a batch task allocator
Zenoh preferred for ROS2; MQTT for lightweight IoT robots

A fleet management system is a high-value target: an attacker who can inject false task assignments or corrupt route plans can cause collisions, damage goods, or halt operations. All FMS-to-robot communication channels should be mutually authenticated (TLS with per-robot client certificates) and integrity-protected. The FMS itself should verify that reported robot positions are physically plausible (e.g., a robot cannot teleport 20 m between two heartbeat messages) before acting on them.

Reliability depends on FMS redundancy and robot-level fallback. Deploy the FMS as a primary-replica pair with automatic failover; replicate all state changes to the replica synchronously before acknowledging them to robots. On each robot, the local navigation stack should be able to execute safe-stop (halt in place) and return-to-charger autonomously without FMS instruction — a robot should never be in a state where FMS failure leaves it in an unsafe condition.
Fleet coordination combines task allocation (who does what), conflict-free routing (who goes where), and deadlock management (who backs off) — all three must work together
Single-item auction (bid = time-to-start) is the practical baseline for task allocation; CBBA extends this to multi-task bundles with distributed consensus
Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) via CBS/ECBS produces conflict-free space-time routes; CBS is optimal but exponential — use PIBT or prioritized planning for large fleets
Deadlocks are detected via cycle detection in the Resource Allocation Graph; resolution via pre-emption of the lowest-cost robot in the cycle
ROS2 multi-robot deployments use per-robot namespaces to isolate topics and services; Open-RMF provides a vendor-neutral fleet management layer on top
The centralized FMS is a single point of failure — deploy as primary-replica, implement local fallback on each robot, and use persistent task state logs for recovery

Production deployments at scale (Ocado, Amazon) exploit fixed grid infrastructure to make MAPF tractable; unstructured environments with dynamic obstacles are harder and require robust local replanning
